does: This paper proposes LaSRO, a method for fine-tuning ultra-fast two-step diffusion models using learned differentiable surrogate rewards in latent space. Rather than directly applying reinforcement learning to step-distilled models, LaSRO learns surrogate reward models within SDXL's latent space that convert arbitrary non-differentiable rewards into differentiable ones suitable for gradient-based optimization. The method uses pre-trained latent diffusion models for reward modeling and implements efficient off-policy exploration tailored to extremely fast image generation. Empirical results demonstrate that LaSRO outperforms existing RL baselines including DDPO and Diffusion-DPO across various reward objectives while maintaining training stability.
connects: This work extends recent research on reward-guided diffusion model fine-tuning, building on the success of RL-based alignment methods like DDPO and DPO that enable flexible model control. However, it identifies and addresses specific limitations of policy-gradient approaches when applied to severely distilled models with two or fewer steps. The paper connects to broader literature on surrogate modeling and value-based RL approaches, learning latent-space representations that bridge the gap between arbitrary rewards and differentiable optimization. It also leverages pre-trained latent diffusion model components, following the trend of efficient reuse of large-scale foundation models for downstream tasks.
enables: LaSRO opens pathways for practical deployment of reward-aligned ultra-fast image generation in resource-constrained settings by demonstrating stable fine-tuning of two-step models. The surrogate reward modeling approach could generalize to other domains beyond images or other heavily distilled generation tasks. This work suggests future research directions around understanding why policy-based RL fails on extreme distillation and whether hybrid value-based approaches offer fundamental advantages. The latent-space surrogate reward framework may enable real-time reward optimization and opens possibilities for hierarchical or curriculum-based training of minimal-step models, potentially extending reward alignment to even single-step generation systems.
